title: Ensure that the Container Network Interface file permissions are set to 600 or more restrictive

input_row.title: Ensure that the Container Network Interface file permissions are set to 600 or more restrictive
input_row.description: Ensure that the Container Network Interface files have permissions of 600 or more restrictive.
input_row.rationale: Container Network Interface provides various networking options for overlay networking. You should consult their documentation and restrict their respective file permissions to maintain the integrity of those files. Those files should be writable by only the administrators on the system. Impact: None
input_row.audit: Run the below command (based on the file location on your system) on the Control Plane node. For example, stat -c %a <path/to/cni/files> Verify that the permissions are 600 or more restrictive.
input_row.remediation: Run the below command (based on the file location on your system) on the Control Plane node. For example, chmod 600 <path/to/cni/files> Default Value: NA References: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/cluster-administration/networking/

step1_initial_assessment:
**AUDIT APPROACH:** Manual

**APPROACH DECISION:**
- This control requires verification of file permissions on the Container Network Interface (CNI) files (e.g., in /etc/cni/net.d/), which reside on the host filesystem of the control plane node.
- Kubernetes APIs and kubectl do not expose host-level file permissions or allow querying of file system attributes for files outside of managed Kubernetes resources.
- The audit procedure explicitly instructs the operator to run OS-level commands (e.g., stat -c %a <path/to/cni/files>) directly on the node.
- This check cannot be performed via Kubernetes API, kubectl, or any controller configuration.
- Therefore, this control requires direct OS access and is not automatable via Kubernetes-native interfaces.

**MANUAL STEPS:**
1. SSH into each Kubernetes control plane node.
2. Identify the directory containing the CNI configuration files (commonly /etc/cni/net.d/).
3. For each file in the CNI directory, run:
   ```
   stat -c %a <path/to/cni/file>
   ```
   or to check all files at once:
   ```
   find /etc/cni/net.d/ -type f -exec stat -c "%n %a" {} \;
   ```
4. Verify that each file's permissions are set to 600 or more restrictive (i.e., only the owner has read/write access).
5. If any file has permissions more permissive than 600 (e.g., 644, 666), this is non-compliant.
6. Remediate by running:
   ```
   chmod 600 <path/to/cni/file>
   ```
   for each non-compliant file.
7. Confirm compliance by re-running the stat/find command and ensuring all files are 600 or more restrictive.

**SOURCE:** CIS KUBERNETES BENCHMARK V1.8.0 PDF
